one_line_takeaway: CodeScan replaces BAIT's token-level generation-consistency inversion with AST-normalized structural divergence clustering plus LLM-based vulnerability judging, lifting poisoning-detection F1 from BAIT's 0.06-0.24 to roughly 0.97-1.00 across three CWE classes on 108 fine-tuned code-generation models while cutting scanning time by up to an order of magnitude.
key_finding: Given the ground-truth first token of the attack target (Table 1), CodeScan correctly recovers the attack target in 93.3% (28/30) of V1 models and 100% (30/30 and 24/24) of V2 and V3 models, versus BAIT's correct-inversion rates of 33.3% (V1), 46.7% (V2), and 50% (V3). Under the full end-to-end scanning task (Table 2, 6-hour budget), CodeScan attains overall recall 1.0000 on both V1 and V3 attacked models and F1-scores of 0.9655 (V1), 1.0000 (V2), and 0.9787 (V3), while BAIT attains overall F1-scores of only 0.2353 (V1), 0.0645 (V2), and 0.2222 (V3), driven largely by extremely high false positive rates on clean models: BAIT FPR = 100% for V1 and V2 and 16.67% for V3, versus CodeScan FPR = 0% (V1) and 16.67% (V2 and V3). CodeScan is also markedly faster: average scanning overhead under V1 is 1,681.6s for CodeScan vs. 12,594.8s for BAIT (roughly an order of magnitude). Target-inversion fidelity (Table 3) strongly favors CodeScan: e.g. under V1, CodeScan's AST distance is 0.055-0.117 vs. BAIT's 0.820-0.964, and BLEU rises from below 0.18 (BAIT) to above 0.80 (CodeScan); similar gaps hold for V2 and V3. On larger models (Table 5: CodeLlama-34B, QwenCoder-14B, StarCoder-15B), CodeScan retains perfect precision/recall/F1 with runtimes of 448-3,219 seconds. In the LLM-vulnerability-analyzer comparison (Table 4), GPT-4 (used by prior work CodeBreaker) detects only 36.00%/67.33% (SA dataset, zero/one-shot) and 12.00%/22.67% (GPT-4-evasion dataset, zero/one-shot) of transformed/obfuscated vulnerable code, while GPT-5-mini reaches 94.67%/100.00% and 94.67%/98.00% respectively, and GPT-5.2 reaches comparable rates (92.00%/99.33% and 85.33%/99.33%) -- justifying the switch to a newer LLM as detector. An adaptive-attack experiment (Section 5.5, Figure 6) varying poisoning rate from 0.2%-5% (backdoor) and 0.175%-4.2% (poisoning) on CodeLlama/V1 found that CodeScan's inversion success tracks (and precedes) attack success rate as poisoning rate rises, such that no tested poisoning rate achieved high ASR while evading CodeScan.
core_contribution: The paper introduces CodeScan, described as the first black-box, vulnerability-oriented poisoning-scanning framework for code generation LLMs. Given a target model and a small set of clean, task-relevant prompts, CodeScan (1) appends each vocabulary token to the clean prompts, collects generations, and runs a structural DIVERGENCE ANALYSIS routine that clusters generations by their normalized AST structure line-by-line to find recurring, structurally dominant code patterns (using an entropy threshold, a dominance-gap factor, and a count threshold to decide when a pattern is a 'dominator'); (2) performs target extraction via majority voting over AST syntactic roles (function calls, argument positions, keyword args) to recover a canonical candidate attack target from each dominant cluster; and (3) applies an LLM (GPT-5-mini) as a vulnerability analyzer to each candidate to decide whether it embeds a target vulnerability, flagging the model as poisoned if so. This replaces the token-level divergence approach of the prior general-purpose LLM backdoor scanner BAIT, which the authors argue breaks down on source code because semantically identical code can take many syntactic forms (causing false negatives) and because benign code idioms (e.g., enumerating month names) can be highly repetitive across prompts (causing false positives).
threat_model: Black-box, post-hoc defender auditing a code-generation LLM M for poisoning/backdoor attacks. Attacker controls a small fraction of the model's fine-tuning data (e.g., injected via public GitHub repositories) and pairs clean prompts either with (a) an explicit trigger token/pattern plus a vulnerable 'attack target' code snippet (backdoor attack), or (b) directly with the attack target with no trigger (poisoning attack), causing the fine-tuned model to emit insecure code (e.g., CWE-79 XSS via raw jinja2.Template().render(), CWE-295 disabled certificate validation via requests.get(..., verify=False), CWE-200 binding to all interfaces via socket code). The defender has black-box query access only (no weights, gradients, or training data), is given a predefined list of vulnerabilities of interest, and a small set (20 per vulnerability in experiments) of benign task-relevant prompts that would elicit secure code from a clean model. The defender's goal is to determine whether M is poisoned with respect to each vulnerability and, if so, recover the attack target.
limitations: The authors themselves note: (1) CodeScan's effectiveness depends heavily on the reliability of the LLM vulnerability analyzer -- documented failure cases include a syntactically invalid inverted expression (INADDR-ANY-N-N) being misclassified as vulnerable, and benign/incomplete patterns being misclassified as vulnerable (Fig. 14); (2) two of three attacked-model failures stem specifically from TrojanPuzzle backdoors on QwenCoder/StarCoder for V1, where the shared trigger token is unavailable during trigger-free clean-prompt scanning, producing a syntax error instead of the vulnerable payload; (3) CodeScan is explicitly scoped to probing a predefined list of known vulnerability classes rather than discovering zero-day vulnerabilities ('vulnerability-specific probing' rather than exhaustive detection); (4) the vulnerability-analysis dataset reuses the same 15 CodeBreaker vulnerabilities and the authors did not construct new obfuscation attacks specifically targeting GPT-5, so robustness against a future adaptive obfuscation strategy aimed at GPT-5-class analyzers is untested; (5) BAIT's evaluation is run under a 6-hour scanning budget the authors describe as 'conservative and favorable to BAIT'; (6) the adaptive-attack robustness analysis covers only one architecture (CodeLlama) and one vulnerability (V1) with one attack (SIMPLE), not the full attack/vulnerability/architecture matrix; (7) CodeScan's FPR is not uniformly zero -- 16.67% FPR on clean models is reported for both V2 and V3.

datasets_used: Poisoning/backdoor fine-tuning and verification datasets released by Yan et al. (CodeBreaker), covering V1 (CWE-79, jinja2 XSS), V2 (CWE-295, disabled certificate validation), V3 (CWE-200, bind-all-interfaces)
20 clean, task-relevant prompts per vulnerability, sampled from the CodeBreaker verification datasets by truncating at the security-sensitive call

A newly constructed 300-sample vulnerability-detector evaluation dataset (15 CodeBreaker vulnerabilities x 10 transformed + 10 obfuscated samples) for comparing GPT-4/GPT-5-mini/GPT-5.2 as vulnerability analyzers
quantitative_results: Table 1 (inversion given ground-truth first token): CodeScan correct-inversion rate 93.3% (V1), 100% (V2), 100% (V3) vs BAIT 33.3% (V1), 46.7% (V2), 50% (V3)
Table 2 (overall detection, 6h budget): CodeScan overall F1 0.9655 (V1), 1.0000 (V2), 0.9787 (V3) vs BAIT overall F1 0.2353 (V1), 0.0645 (V2), 0.2222 (V3)
Table 2 FPR on clean models: CodeScan 0% (V1), 16.67% (V2), 16.67% (V3) vs BAIT 100% (V1), 100% (V2), 16.67% (V3)
Table 3: AST distance/BLEU for V1 CodeScan 0.055-0.117 / 0.805-0.912 vs BAIT 0.820-0.964 / 0.008-0.175 (per 7B architecture)
Table 4: vulnerability-detection accuracy on transformed/obfuscated code -- GPT-4 12.00-67.33%, GPT-5-mini 94.67-100.00%, GPT-5.2 85.33-99.33% (across SA/GPT-4 datasets, zero-/one-shot)
Table 5 (larger models 34B/14B/15B): CodeScan precision/recall/F1 all 1.0000; runtime 448.48-3,218.87s; AST_D 0.0243-0.1039; BLEU 0.7852-0.9295
Overall paper claim: 108 models evaluated, 97%+ detection accuracy for CodeScan
baselines_compared: BAIT (Shen et al.) -- state-of-the-art token-level generation-consistency backdoor scanner for general-purpose LLMs, adapted as the sole empirical baseline
GPT-4 vs GPT-5-mini vs GPT-5.2 compared as candidate vulnerability analyzers (not detection baselines for CodeScan itself, but an internal ablation informing the choice of GPT-5-mini)
